preamble: Debuccalization of the voiceless velar fricative /x/ to the glottal fricative /h/ is a common form of lenition. By moving the site of friction from the soft palate (velar) to the glottis, the speaker reduces the articulatory effort. This process is highly productive in Spanish dialects and has occurred historically in Germanic languages.

examples.note: The standard velar fricative /x/ (written as 'j') debuccalizes to [h] in much of Andalusia, the Canary Islands, and the Americas.
The Middle English velar fricative [x] (written 'gh') weakened to [h] before being lost in most positions.

sources: Penny, R. (2002). A History of the Spanish Language.
Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
